SWORD (Southwest Ohio Regional Depository) is a closed-stacks storage depository, not a public-access library -- it has no public walk-in hours and can't be browsed in person [1]. Materials stored there are requested through interlibrary loan and delivered to your campus library for pickup [2]. For reference, SWORD is located at 4200 N. Search course reserves in Primo: type the textbook title, a course abbreviation (e.g. ECO 201), or your professor's last name [1]. Loan periods for reserve items are chosen by the instructor -- 2-hour checkout for use in the library, 1-day, or 3-day -- and all reserve materials are removed at the end of every semester [1]. The reserves guide has the full details, including how instructors place materials on reserve [1].

It covers our books, ebooks, articles, media, and OhioLINK partner libraries. A librarian on Ask Us can also help you find materials.

To find a specific book, article, journal, or DVD at Miami University Libraries, search Primo -- the library catalog. It searches across our physical collection, ebooks, journal articles, and OhioLINK partner libraries in one place.

Primo: https://ohiolink-mu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/search?vid=01OHIOLINK_MU:MU

If Primo says "no results" and you think the library should have it, you can request it through Interlibrary Loan: https://www.lib.miamioh.edu/use/borrow/ill/

To find articles or browse research databases, use the library's Databases A-Z list. It's the authoritative index of every database the library subscribes to -- organized alphabetically and by subject -- and links directly into each database with proper authentication.

Why send you there instead of looking up a specific database name? Database names overlap and shift ("Web of Science" vs "Web of Knowledge", "PsycINFO" vs "Psychology and Behavioral Sciences Collection"); the A-Z page disambiguates them with current names + subject tags.

Databases A-Z: https://libguides.lib.miamioh.edu/az/databases

Use the Miami Libraries Databases A-Z list to find current access options [1]. The source points to A-Z for EBSCO rather than confirming a specific platform name, so that page is the right place to check access [1].

For current events, exhibits, and library news, please visit the News & Events page directly: https://www.lib.miamioh.edu/about/news-events/news/

King Library's quiet/silent floor is on the THIRD FLOOR [1].

Yes -- King Library has lockers in the Reading Rooms. They are restricted to active faculty and actively enrolled graduate students. Locker assignments are requested via an online form on the Reading Rooms page and are assigned yearly on a first-come, first-served basis (with a waitlist when full) [1].

Miami University Libraries' building policies -- food and drink, alcohol, sleeping/napping, noise, pets and service animals, smoking/vaping, bikes and skateboards, and more -- are in the Libraries' Facilities & Events Policies guide [1].
Use the Printing & WiFi page for the current laptop printing instructions [1]. That page is the canonical Oxford printing page, so it carries the up-to-date steps [1].
Use the Printing & WiFi page for current WiFi connection details [1].
Yes, color printing is available. For current details on printing and location, use the Printing & WiFi page [1].
You can scan a document at King Library in Oxford; it has scanners [1]. For current printing/scanning details, see the Printing & WiFi page [2].
Yes — you can borrow a Chromebook laptop for 30 days [1]. The tech checkout page says to come to a library checkout desk with your university ID to borrow equipment [1].
Chromebook laptops may be checked out for 30 days [1].
Yes — the libraries offer charger cables to borrow, including chargers for Mac/PC/phones and other peripherals [1]. You can check availability at the tech equipment checkout page [1].

For Oxford campus library computers, see the “Software on Library Computers” page for the current list of available software [1]. It includes Windows and Mac software available on student/faculty/staff-use computers in the libraries [1].
Yes. The software page says additional titles such as MATLAB can be added to library computers using Software Center on campus Windows PCs and Self-Service on campus Macs [1].

Yes. The software page says additional titles such as MATLAB and SPSS can be added to library computers using the Software Center on campus PCs, or the Self-Service software install on campus Macs [1].

Use the Miami University Libraries citation resources for APA rather than generating the website citation inline [1]. The APA citation resources page is here: https://libguides.lib.miamioh.edu/citation/apa [1].
Use the APA citation page on the citation LibGuide [1]. It points you to citation resources, which include recommended generators and tools [2][1].
Yes — the citation help resources include Zotero tutorials [1]. For research help, you can also use Ask Us to chat, email, make an appointment, or call [2].
Yes -- librarians offer research consultations. Find the subject librarian for your course, major, or topic on the subject librarians page and contact them directly to set up an appointment [1].

For help with research strategy for your paper, use the Miami University Libraries “Ask a Librarian” page to connect with library staff (including options for in-person help, email, and phone) [1]. You can also schedule a research consultation/appointment through the Ask Us page [1]. For more specialized help, find and contact your subject librarian via the Subject Liaison Librarians page [2].

You can get research help through the Miami University Libraries “Ask a Librarian” service, which offers live online assistance, in-person help at an MU library, email, and phone support [1]. For phone help, you can call King Library at (513) 529-4141 or Wertz Art/Architecture Library at (513) 529-6638 [1]. If you need more in-depth help (e.g., research strategy or a dissertation literature review), use the Ask Us page to book a research consultation/appointment or connect to a librarian through the “talk to a human librarian” option [1].

Yes. Miami University Libraries (Oxford) offers Data Services, including consultations for data analysis (including R and Python), GIS help, and data visualization support [1]. You can also use the shortcut URL https://www.lib.miamioh.edu/GetMeData/ to reach the Data Services page [2].

Yes — Oxford Library data services offers consultations, including GIS help and data visualization support [1]. The GIS resources article also says library staff are happy to help with GIS projects and points to Eric Johnson / the Center for Digital Scholarship for more information [2].
Oxford Library data services can help with data analysis through consultations, including support for R, Python, and statistical analysis [1]. For details, use the data services page [1].

Yes. Miami University librarians provide in-class instruction, workshops, and individual consultations, and the instruction page says to fill out the Library Instruction Request Form to schedule a session [1].

To schedule a library instruction session, fill out the Library Instruction Request Form on the Library Instruction page [1]. The page says the form is used to schedule a session with a librarian [1].
For the Dean of University Libraries and the library administration/leadership team, see the Dean's Office page [1].
The Libraries' staff directory is on the staff page -- you can look up any staff member and their contact information there [1].
Alea Henle is the Head, Access & Borrow Department [1], and that department encompasses circulation [1]. The staff directory page is the place to look up the current head of circulation [2].
No. King Library is Closed on Saturday (2026-07-18) [1].

That's further out than I can look up live -- my hours check only covers the near term, and the schedule shifts by term, break, and holiday, so I can't reliably tell you that date myself. The library's hours page always shows the current and upcoming schedule, so please check the date you need there: https://www.lib.miamioh.edu/about/locations/hours/.

Library hours vary by building and by term, so none of the libraries are routinely open 24 hours. King Library is the only building that runs near-24-hour schedules, and only during finals periods. Please check the hours page for the building and date you need [1].

Yes—recalls/holds are placed through your Miami University Libraries account in OhioLINK/Primo (the bot can’t submit a recall for you) [1]. Log in to “My Account” to place the request: https://ohiolink-mu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/account?vid=01OHIOLINK_MU:MU&section=overview&lang=en [1]. For recall/request details and policies, see the Miami University Libraries circulation policies/recall-request pages [2][3].

For the policy on lost books (including any replacement/processing fees and the replacement procedure), use Miami University Libraries’ circulation policies page here: https://libguides.lib.miamioh.edu/mul-circulation-policies/loan-periods-fines [1][2]. It’s also a good idea to contact the circulation desk at the library you borrowed the item from for help with next steps [3].

Miami University Libraries does not issue an alumni library card. For the borrowing options currently available after graduation, please check the circulation policies page [1], or ask the circulation desk at (513) 529-4141.
The library holds a book on the hold shelf for typically 7 days after it’s ready for pickup [1]. If you need help after that, the circulation policies page is the place to check [1].
After hours, return books to the exterior drop boxes at the main entrance(s) of King Library [1][2]. King Library is the Oxford location at 151 S. Campus Ave, Oxford, OH 45056 [1].
Yes -- scanners is available at all three campuses: Oxford (King), Hamilton (Rentschler), and Middletown (Gardner-Harvey). [1][2][3]
Hamilton’s Rentschler Library has course reserves [1][2]. For Hamilton-specific course reserves details, use the library services page [1][2].

I'm focused on Miami University Libraries questions -- hours, services, spaces, research help, and policies. The question you asked is outside that scope, so I don't have a reliable answer for it.

On the Oxford campus, King Library has a designated quiet/silent floor on the THIRD FLOOR [1]. King also offers Reading Rooms (including a Faculty and Graduate Reading Room) as additional quiet study options [2].
Yes — printing is offered at Wertz Art & Architecture Library [1]. For the location details, Wertz is in Alumni Hall, Oxford, OH 45056 [1].
Use the Printing & WiFi page for the library’s current scan options and scanner instructions [1]. It also shows where you can scan at the Oxford libraries [1].

Microfilm at King is handled through the Walter Havighurst Special Collections, which King Library houses [1]. The King Library page also says King offers “computer labs and printing/scanning” and houses Special Collections, so use the King location page and Special Collections materials/services pages for the current viewing and request instructions [1].
I don't have a reliable answer to that. You can ask a librarian directly through Ask Us.
Yes — headphones are listed among the equipment available for checkout at the Oxford libraries [1][2]. You can borrow equipment at a library checkout desk with your university ID [1][2].

King Library is not open after midnight today; the posted hours for Thursday (2026-07-16) are **7:30am to 9:00pm** [1]. If you’re asking about late-night/after-hours access at other times (e.g., finals), check the current schedule on the Library Hours page, since hours can vary by term [1].

The King Library location information page is the place Miami University Libraries provides details about spaces and amenities in King Library [1]. Please check that page for any current information about a nursing room or lactation space in the library [1].
Check the King Library location page for restroom locations by floor [1]. If you’re in the building, the service desk or floor signage can also help you find them [1].

King Library’s location page is the place to check for floor descriptions and location details [1]. If you’re looking for a graphical floor plan or map, the King Library page is the relevant starting point [1].
Yes—King Library has study seating, and the location page says outlets are widely available there [1]. If you need a charger, King also has technology and computing equipment checkout [1].

Miami University Libraries' building policies -- food and drink, alcohol, sleeping/napping, noise, pets and service animals, smoking/vaping, bikes and skateboards, and more -- are in the Libraries' Facilities & Events Policies guide [1].
Use Miami University Libraries’ citation guide for MLA help [1]. The guide covers MLA among other citation styles, so it’s the right place to find the current website-citation instructions [1].

For Chicago Manual of Style help, use the Libraries’ Citation LibGuide: https://libguides.lib.miamioh.edu/citation [1]. You can also contact a subject librarian who specializes in history/humanities for Chicago-style help (start via Ask a Librarian options: chat/email/appointment/phone) [1][2][1].

Most article databases (EBSCO, JSTOR, and others) have a 'peer-reviewed' or 'scholarly journals' checkbox filter -- apply it to limit your results to peer-reviewed articles. Pick a database from the Databases A-Z list [1], and if you're not sure which database fits your topic, your subject librarian can recommend one [2].

Miami University Libraries has a Miami University Theses & Dissertation collection in its digital collections [1]. The operator-verified note for the digital-collections page says to point to the OhioLINK ETD Center / Miami's institutional repository (Scholarly Commons) for theses and dissertations [1].
Your subject librarian is Jenny Presnell (presnejl@miamioh.edu) [1].

The tech checkout page lists equipment available for checkout and says you can borrow items at a library checkout desk with your university ID [1]. It does not list HDMI cables in the provided equipment snippet, so use the tech-checkout page for the current equipment list [1].

The tech-checkout page says the libraries offer equipment you can check out, but the equipment list shown here does not specifically mention graphing calculators [1]. For current availability, check the Tech Equipment for Checkout page [1].
DSLR cameras are checked out as 24-hour items on the Oxford tech checkout page [1].

Miami University Libraries’ Tech Equipment for Checkout page lists the technology items you can borrow and is the best place to confirm whether USB drives are available for checkout [1]. To check availability or ask about specific items, you can call Edward King Library at 513-529-4141 [1][2].

Yes — the Libraries list chargers and cables for phones/laptops as available for checkout at the circulation desk with a Miami ID [1]. The tech-checkout page is the place to confirm the current borrowable charger options [2][1].

I can’t file a lost-and-found report for you, but you can report a lost item at the King Library circulation/service desk [1]. You can also contact the Libraries through Ask Us for help connecting with the right place [2].

I don't have access to your library account. Please check your account at https://ohiolink-mu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/account?vid=01OHIOLINK_MU:MU&section=overview&lang=en or contact us at (513) 529-4141.

Search course reserves in Primo: type the textbook title, a course abbreviation (e.g. ECO 201), or your professor's last name [1]. Loan periods for reserve items are chosen by the instructor -- 2-hour checkout for use in the library, 1-day, or 3-day -- and all reserve materials are removed at the end of every semester [1]. The reserves guide has the full details, including how instructors place materials on reserve [1].

I can't submit ILL requests for you -- requests go through the official form so they're tied to your account. Submit yours at https://www.lib.miamioh.edu/use/borrow/ill/ (takes a couple of minutes). Pickup is at your campus library.

I don't have access to your library account. Please check your account at https://ohiolink-mu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/account?vid=01OHIOLINK_MU:MU&section=overview&lang=en or contact us at (513) 529-4141.
3D printing at the King Library MakerSpace is often free of charge, but rates can change -- please check the current pricing on the MakerSpace guide before you print [1].

Miami University Libraries’ software checkout provides access to Adobe Creative Cloud for Miami students and for faculty/staff through separate checkout/reservation flows [1]. The Software page describes faculty/staff access (e.g., reserving an Adobe Acrobat Pro license for faculty/staff, and then logging in at adobe.com with Miami credentials) and notes that students can check out the full Adobe Creative Cloud suite [1]. It does not explicitly state whether part-time/contingent staff are eligible, so the best next step is to use the Software Checkout information on the software page to confirm your eligibility and access path [1].

Special Collections uses appointment-only research access [1][2]. For handling materials, use the Special Collections reading-room rules on the Special Collections page, which include pencils only, no food or drink, and supervised access [1][3].
Miami provides New York Times access for affiliated users. The Libraries' New York Times guide has the current activation steps — see [1].

Miami's digital exhibits and collections are listed on the Digital Collections site -- I can't reliably confirm coverage of a specific topic from here, so please browse the collections (and the past digital exhibit archive linked there) to see what's available [1].
